seo.title: Dr. Leiza Hollas — Cardiovascular Surgeon
seo.description: Dr. Leiza Loiane Hollas, cardiovascular surgeon, member of SBCCV. Specialized in cardiac surgery, valve surgery, and surgical simulation. Based in Curitiba, PR.

sobre.altImagem: Photo of Dr. Leiza Loiane Hollas
sobre.texto:
Cardiovascular surgeon and member of the Brazilian Society of Cardiovascular Surgery (SBCCV), she focuses her practice on the surgical treatment of heart diseases, prioritizing safety, technical precision, and individualized care.

She completed her medical residency in Cardiovascular Surgery at the Brazilian Red Cross Hospital – Paraná (2024), consolidating specialized training in high-complexity procedures and integrated cardiovascular patient management.

Graduated in Medicine from the Pontifical Catholic University of Paraná (2018), her practice is mainly focused on cardiovascular surgery, valve surgery, and surgical simulation, constantly seeking to combine innovation, scientific evidence, and excellence in patient care.

In addition to her clinical and surgical work, she maintains continuous scientific activity, with articles published in national and international journals. She is a member of The Valve Club, an initiative dedicated to disseminating medical knowledge through the discussion of studies related to valvular heart disease and structural heart disease — fields that are constantly evolving in modern cardiology.

She believes that cardiovascular surgery requires not only technical mastery but also attentive listening and responsibility in every decision. For this reason, her work is guided by building a relationship of trust with each patient, providing individualized care throughout every stage of treatment.

card1.texto: Maintain absolute fasting for 8 hours before surgery, including water.

card2.texto: Inform your doctor about all medications used and known allergies.

card3.texto: Bring a photo ID, recent tests, and insurance authorization if applicable.

card4.texto: Take a shower with antiseptic soap the night before and the day of surgery.

card1.texto: Confirm your full name before any exams or procedures. Keep the ID bracelet until discharge.

card2.texto: Do not provide data or make payments over the phone. Contact the hospital's cashier for questions.

card1.texto: Even if you feel fine, call the nursing team before getting up.

card2.texto: Keep personal items within reach and the call button accessible.

card1.texto: After using the bathroom, before meals, and when entering or leaving the hospital. Use 70% alcohol if not visibly dirty.

card2.texto: Ask companions and visitors to clean their hands frequently too.

card1.texto: AAS, Clopidogrel, Marevan, Marcoumar, Eliquis must be suspended 5 days before surgery. Rivaroxaban, 2 days before. Always follow medical advice!

card2.texto: Check in the day before surgery at 2 PM. Bring documents, tests, authorization, and personal items.

card3.texto: Remove false eyelashes, gel nails, piercings, spacers, hair extensions, and valuables.

card4.texto: After insurance approval, schedule the pre-anesthetic evaluation in advance.

card1.texto: Yes, one companion over 18 is allowed during hospitalization.

card2.texto: Bringing outside food is not allowed without permission from the nutrition team.

card3.texto: Visits are allowed from 2 PM to 8 PM. Under 16s are not allowed in ICUs.

card4.titulo: How to speak with the medical team?
card4.texto: Medical information is given by the responsible doctor to the legal guardian.

theValveClub.descricao: The Valve Club platform aims to disseminate knowledge and science through articles related to valvular heart disease and structural heart disease — a growing area of interest in cardiology, as well as a subject that sparks curiosity in those who interact with patients affected by these conditions.

livro.titulo: Basic Manual of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery from LACTCV
livro.resumo: The LACTCV Basic Manual of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery introduces essential fundamentals of the specialty—from anatomy to the most common conditions—through a clear and accessible didactic approach.
livro.conteudo:
The LACTCV Basic Manual of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery was developed to introduce readers to the essential fundamentals of thoracic and cardiovascular surgery, covering topics from anatomical concepts to the most frequent pathologies in the specialty.

The purpose of this work is to provide didactic, objective, and accessible content, especially aimed at medical students and trainees, offering a solid foundation for understanding the field's core principles.

Throughout its pages, readers are guided through a learning journey that brings together technical knowledge, conceptual clarity, and practical application, serving as a starting point for continuous development in a specialty that demands precision, dedication, and constant growth.

This book is an invitation to study and to build a professional path grounded in excellence and commitment to healthcare.
livro.palavrasChave: 1. Heart – Surgery. I. Hollas, Leiza Loiane (Organizer). II. Manoel, Agnes Zanotto (Organizer). III. Iwamoto, Aiko (Organizer). IV. Title.

artigos.subtitulo: Is every procedure a little genius and a little madness?
artigos.resumo: The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population and, given the high prevalence, there is currently great medical interest in seeking interventional strategies — both conventional surgical and minimally invasive (transcatheter).
artigos.conteudo:
The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population and, given the high prevalence, there is currently great medical interest in seeking interventional strategies — both conventional surgical and minimally invasive (transcatheter) — that can be indicated for patients with valvular heart disease, aiming to reduce the morbidity and mortality associated with this condition.

In recent years, transcatheter technologies have gained prominence in the treatment of symptomatic mitral valve insufficiency. However, it is important to note that not all patients with this condition are candidates for these 'less invasive' interventions.

According to the latest Brazilian Valvular Heart Disease Guideline (2020), the percutaneous method should be reserved for patients with moderate-to-severe or severe mitral insufficiency (MI), of both primary and secondary etiologies, with refractory symptoms and high or prohibitive surgical risk.

But what is MitraClip®? Transcatheter mitral valve repair, also known as TEER (Transcatheter Edge-to-Edge Repair), uses this percutaneous device to perform an approach similar to the 'Alfieri' surgical procedure, formerly called edge-to-edge repair, created in 1991 by Italian surgeon Ottavio Alfieri, but in a minimally invasive manner.

With this technique, a functional — not anatomical — correction of the mitral valve is performed, by fixing A2 to P2 through sutures, resulting in a double-orifice mitral valve. This technique is effective in situations of double prolapse (of both anterior and posterior mitral valve leaflets) and in Barlow disease (in which the leaflets are thickened and redundant).

In these situations of exuberant leaflets, the Alfieri technique aims, in addition to reducing the regurgitant volume and restoring mitral valve function, to prevent the high mobility of the anterior leaflet and the risks of systolic anterior motion postoperatively. When performed in situations of isolated P2 prolapse, or less exuberant leaflets, care must be taken not to induce mitral stenosis.

During the MitraClip® procedure, the device is inserted through catheters in the femoral vein and guided to the mitral valve (MV) through a transseptal puncture of the interatrial septum. The MitraClip® is then used to 'clip' the mitral leaflets at the medial scallops, creating approximation and reducing the valve regurgitation orifice.

Using data from the CUTTING-EDGE Registry (an international retrospective registry of patients undergoing mitral valve surgery after TEER failure), the study 'Impact of Mitral Regurgitation Etiology on Mitral Surgery After Transcatheter Edge-to-Edge Repair' compared the outcome of mitral valve surgery after failure of percutaneous treatment with MitraClip® in patients with primary versus secondary MI.

A total of 330 patients were analyzed from July 2009 to July 2020, of whom 47% had primary MI and 53% had secondary MI. The mean interval between the TEER procedure and mitral valve surgery was 3.5 months (IQR: 0.5–11.6 months), and indications for surgery included: recurrent MI (33.6%), residual MI (28.8%), leaflet insertion loss (25.2%), partial clip detachment (21.5%), mitral stenosis (14.5%), and clip embolization (2.1%). One-year mortality after surgery was 31.3% overall, being higher in patients with secondary MI (38.3% vs 23.2%; P < 0.019).

Furthermore, this group also demonstrated lower cumulative 3-year survival after surgery, possibly due to a greater comorbidity burden and consequently higher surgical risk. Patients with secondary mitral regurgitation also had more aborted TEER procedures (25.7% vs. 16.3%; p=0.043), which is concerning, since overall mortality was higher in this group (aborted: 47.7%, acute failure: 28.9%, late failure: 26.7%; p=0.032).

It is worth noting that among patients undergoing the percutaneous procedure, 46.9% of those with low and moderate preoperative surgical risk make up the primary mitral regurgitation (PMR) group, while 49% are in the secondary etiology group (SMR). Among the main comorbidities present in the PMR and SMR groups: associated coronary artery disease was present in 42.6% of the first group and 58.3% of the second; diabetes mellitus in 27.3% of PMR and 35.8% of SMR; and chronic kidney disease in 52.4% of PMR and 62.3% of SMR.

Given these comorbidities, combined with the ventricular structural disease generally present in secondary mitral regurgitation, surgical reapproach becomes more dangerous in this group. Technically, the key points involve highlighting the main etiology of primary mitral insufficiency — myxomatous degeneration with thickening and redundancy of the valve leaflets — which presents favorable anatomy for clipping.

In secondary mitral insufficiency, the mechanism of mitral regurgitation results from ventricular structural disease rather than leaflet pathology, meaning there is more commonly a failure of leaflet coaptation, making the transcatheter clipping technique more challenging and potentially leading to leaflet tension when joined.

These data show that we must seek satisfactory results after TEER, with the aim of avoiding future complications and ensuring the best quality of life for the patient. Understanding the risks involved in mitral valve surgery after TEER failure highlights the importance of a multidisciplinary Heart Team approach for careful evaluation of patients who may or may not benefit from percutaneous therapy. Furthermore, the experience and skill of the operators performing TEER also play an important role in achieving favorable outcomes.

artigos.resumo: The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population. When valve replacement is necessary, we must be attentive to the occurrence of structural degeneration of bioprostheses, a diagnosis often made late, which can lead to clinical deterioration and ventricular function impairment.
artigos.conteudo:
The increasing incidence of structural valvular heart disease accompanies the progressive aging of the population and, given the high prevalence of rheumatic fever, with consequent involvement of young patients, the demand for valve intervention is directly impacted.

When valve replacement is necessary, especially when choosing a bioprosthesis, we must be attentive to the occurrence of its structural degeneration, a diagnosis often made late, which can lead to clinical deterioration and ventricular function impairment.

According to American College of Cardiology (ACC) guidelines, transthoracic echocardiographic control after valve replacement is suggested at 1, 3, and 12 months after the procedure, and then annually. According to the new 2020 Update of the Brazilian Valvular Heart Disease Guidelines, periodic echocardiographic control after valve replacement is recommended, but the frequency is not specified, leaving the regularity of echocardiogram performance at the discretion of the attending physician.

During this follow-up, active search should be conducted for functional class changes and new symptoms that may indicate bioprosthetic dysfunction.

These dysfunctions can be classified into stages, initially with identification of valve dysfunction without hemodynamic changes, up to more advanced stages, where there is prosthetic dysfunction associated with more severe hemodynamic changes.

They can also be classified into categories: structural dysfunction (calcification or rupture of leaflets) or non-structural (paraprosthetic leak, pannus, thrombi, endocarditis, and mismatch). Of the available diagnostic exams, echocardiography (transthoracic or transesophageal) is the main ally.

The following parameters must be analyzed: left ventricular outflow tract diameter, stroke volume, peak flow velocity, and valve area, as from these data it is possible to calculate parameters such as the Doppler velocity index (DVI), velocity time integral (VTI), and valve area per body surface area, essential in follow-up and classification of prosthetic dysfunction severity.

When analyzing a bioprosthesis in aortic position, its dysfunction is classified as follows (based on echocardiography performed between 1 and 3 months after the procedure):

[[imagem1]]

In cases of doubt or to improve analysis of possible aortic bioprosthetic thrombosis, computed tomography angiography can be used. Findings such as hypoattenuation with leaflet thickening greater than 2 mm (HALT) and reduction in leaflet motion (RELM) support the diagnosis of prosthetic valve thrombosis.

When infectious endocarditis is suspected, with Duke criteria met and without echocardiographic changes, even on transesophageal echocardiography, there is still no consensus, but studies show utility in using positron emission computed tomography (PET-CT) with fluorodeoxyglucose-18.

We should also remember that, in echocardiographic suspicion of aortic prosthetic valve dysfunction with normal CT, we can resort to pressure measurements by hemodynamic analysis.

Unlike the aortic position, the pressure gradient in the mitral position is highly variable from patient to patient (as it is dependent on flow and chronotropy), therefore, cineangiographic analysis is not indicated. The recommendation is to use DVI changes as the main criterion to suspect valve deterioration and determine the evolution of mitral bioprosthetic dysfunction (performing echocardiography between 1 and 3 months after the procedure):

[[imagem2]]

The parameters and criteria for defining structural bioprosthetic dysfunction, valve thrombosis, and endocarditis are similar for both mitral and aortic positions. The greatest examples of non-structural mitral bioprosthetic dysfunction are: paraprosthetic leak, pannus overgrowth, inadequate positioning (creep), and mismatch.

Mismatch can be defined by a series of clinical combinations: prosthesis small relative to the patient's body surface area, stenotic indexed effective orifice, or increased transvalvular gradient after surgery. The indexed effective orifice area (iEOA) is one way to measure the risk of prosthesis-patient mismatch: for the aortic position, if greater than 0.85 cm²/m², the chance of mismatch is practically zero; between 0.65-0.85 cm²/m² there is moderate mismatch; and if less than 0.65 cm²/m², the patient is classified with severe mismatch.

For the mitral position, mismatch is defined as an indexed valve area (iVA) ≤1.2 cm²/m², and severe refers to an iVA ≤0.9 cm²/m². It is noteworthy that, if severe, mitral mismatch is associated with reduced survival and failure of pulmonary hypertension regression.

The objective of structural valve interventions is to optimize hemodynamic load, reduce symptoms, improve quality of life, and possibly prolong survival. In treatment decisions, the limitations and preferences of each patient, the durability and risk of bioprosthetic deterioration, and the feasibility of reoperation if necessary must all be considered.

Finally, not every bioprosthetic dysfunction will have reintervention as the final treatment, often requiring medical management and lifestyle changes. The universe of structural valvular heart disease is complex and requires a multidisciplinary and experienced team, in order to offer the patient safe and effective approaches.

[[imagem3]]

artigos.subtitulo: A duel to tame aortic aneurysms!
artigos.resumo: In 1900, Dr. William Osler already pointed out: 'there is no disease more conducive to clinical humility than aortic aneurysms'. To this day, aortic diseases are a clinical-surgical challenge.
artigos.conteudo:
In 1900, Dr. William Osler already pointed out a medical truth: 'there is no disease more conducive to clinical humility than aortic aneurysms'. Osler's statement is true, as to this day, aortic diseases are a clinical-surgical challenge for the medical field.

It is worth remembering that, on average, the normal diameter of the ascending aorta is about 30mm and dilations above 50% of this value are already considered outside the normal pattern. There are several predisposing factors: smoking, hypertension, genetic disorders (Marfan and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome), infectious (syphilis), and congenital.

Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital alteration, present in 1 to 2% of the world population and is related to thoracic aortic aneurysms in 50% of cases. The risk of developing an aneurysm is up to 0.1% per year and corresponds to a pathology with high mortality.

It is established that patients with ascending aortic aneurysm with diameter ≥ 55mm require surgical intervention. The most practiced surgery is Bentall and De-Bono, which consists of combined replacement of the valve, aortic root, and ascending aorta. However, patients will always be subject to anticoagulation or bioprosthetic deterioration.

As a surgical alternative, there are two techniques involving the 'valve sparing' concept: the Yacoub technique, created in 1982, in which new sinuses of Valsalva are made using Dacron tube, and the David technique, idealized in 1992, in which the tube is implanted directly on the aortic valve annulus with reimplantation of the coronary artery ostia.

A recent systematic review compared the durability and safety of David and Yacoub procedures in patients with bicuspid aortic valve. 1159 patients were included (87.4% male, mean age 44.9 years). Mean mortality was 1.7% at thirty days. Kaplan-Meier survival estimate at 5, 10, and 15 years was 96%, 90%, and 87%, while the estimate of patients free from reoperation in the same period was 96%, 91%, and 88%.

Even in patients with bicuspid aortic valve, preservation of the native aortic valve proves to be clearly more advantageous over replacement, as it presents more physiological hemodynamics, relief from the need for anticoagulation, and greater durability.

artigos.resumo: Intervention in tricuspid valvopathy has clear indication when it occurs concomitantly with mitral and aortic valvopathy intervention. When present in isolation, intervention indication is still controversial.
artigos.conteudo:
Intervention in tricuspid valvopathy has clear indication when it occurs concomitantly with mitral and aortic valvopathy intervention. When present in isolation, the indication for intervention is still controversial among surgeons and cardiologists, with more prevalence in the presence of valvular stenosis.

It is common knowledge that tricuspid valve diseases lead to hemodynamic changes in the cardiac cycle that, depending on their severity, can lead to right heart failure, a severe clinical picture with high morbidity and mortality.

When compared to isolated surgical treatment of aortic and mitral valves, isolated tricuspid valve intervention presents operative mortality 2 to 3 times higher, around 8 to 9%.

A recent meta-analysis with 15,069 patients undergoing isolated tricuspid valve surgery compared valve repair with valve replacement. Results favored valve repair regarding postoperative outcomes, such as atrioventricular blocks, need for permanent pacemaker implantation, mortality, and lower incidence of renal insufficiency.

Therefore, knowing the pathophysiology of tricuspid valvopathy and the correct indication for transcatheter or surgical interventions remain topics of broad debate and importance. Only then will the nickname 'forgotten valve' be resigned to the past.
